content.full_text:
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 ••• 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814 
Tech nical Assistance 
(916) 322·5662 
Ms. Janet Cornelius 
Commission on Aging for 
Yuba/Sutter Counties 
725 D Street 
Marysville, CA 
95901 
Dear Ms. Cornelius: 
Administration 
322·5660 
Executive/legal 
322·5901 
June 23, 1982 
Re: 
A-82-l04 
Enforcement 
322~441 
Statements of Economic r nten 
322-6444 
Thank you for your letter requesting information about 
the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform 
Act. 
The facts, as ypu presented them to me in your letter 
and our phone call, aie that you are currently a paid 
employee of Yuba/Sutter Legal Center, a private, non-profit 
corporation. 
Yo~r duties are varied and include seeking 
and applying for grants. 
You are also serving as the 
Secretary of the Commission on Aging for Yuba and Sutter 
Counties. 
This Commission, in part, makes decisions on 
whether the Legal Center receives certain grants. 
Your question is whether the Political Reform Act 
prevents you from being Secretary of the Commission on 
Ag ing. 
The Political Reform Act does not prevent you from 
holding the position of Secretary, rather it affects your 
ability to make and participate in the making of certain 
decisions. 
Government Code Section 871001/ 
(copy 
enclosed) prohibits a public official from making or 
participating in the making of any decision in whicn tne 
official knows or has reason to know that he or she has a 
financial interest. 
An official has a financial interest 
in a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the 
decision will have a material financial effect on any 
source of income aggregating $250 or more received by the 
puolic official within 12 months prior to the time when the 
11 
Hereinafter all references are to the Government 
Code unless otherwise indicat 


Ms. Janet Cornelius 
June 23, 1982 
Page Two 
decision is made. 
(Section 87103(c), copy enclosed.) 
You 
indicated that the Legal Center is a source of income to 
you. 
In situations where a source of income is a non-profit 
corporation, such as the Legal Center, a decision has a 
material financial effect on the source of income if the 
effect of the decision on the source of income is a 
"significant one." 
(2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 
18702(b) (3) (D).) 
A determination as to whether an effect 
is "significant" can only be made in the context of a 
specific situation, after considering all of the facts. 
However, you stated on the telephone that, in your position 
as Commission Secretary, you may be asked to make or 
participate in the making of decisions concerning grants to 
the Legal Center. 
Considering that the Center is 
non-profit and that it apparently operates on a small 
budget, a decision as to whether to give the Center a grant 
seems like the type of decision which could easily have a 
significant effect on the Center. 
As stated before, the 
need for disqualification depends on the specific facts of 
a decision. 
However, it appears that, in most instances, 
decisions regarding grants for the Legal Center require 
your disqualification. 
You are also required to disqualify yourself from 
making or participating in decisions which will directly or 
indirectly increase or decrease the amount of income you 
receive by $100 or more. 
(Section 18702(b) (3) (A), copy 
enclosed.) 
If a decision on a grant to the Legal Center 
could affect the amount of money available to pay you for 
your work at the Center, resulting in an increase or 
decrease in your salary of $100 or more, you are required 
to abstain from participating in the decis10n. 
Lastly, you are required to disqualify yourself from 
making or participating in decisions if there 1S a nexus 
between the governmental decision and the purposes for 
which you receive income from the Legal Center. 2/ 
(2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18702 (b) (3) (8), copy enclosed.) 
You stated that part of your job is to attempt to generate 
grants for the Center. 
If you vote on a decision to 
2/' 
See the enclosed copy of the Gunther letter of 
March 23, 1982, for further aiscussion of the "nexus test." 


Ms. Janet Cornelius 
June 23, 1982 
Page Three 
provide a grant to the Center, you are accomplishing as a 
public official what you are paid to do in your position 
with the Legal Center. 
This satisfies the "nexus test" and 
disqualification is required. 
I hope I have clarified the applicable provisions of 
the Political Reform Act for you. 
If you have any additional questions, please feel free 
to contact me at 916/322-5901. 
JSM:km 
Enclosures 
Very truly yours, 


~ 
r~ 
r\ 
v-
Gorltmission on Aging 
MAY Z7 
Ii 18 AM '6L 
for Yuba/Sutter Counties 
May 24, 1982 
Fair Political Practices Commission 
1100 K Street, 2nd Floor 
Sacramento, CA 
95814 
Dear Sir/Madam: 
Our Commission has a question that we are hoping you 
can answer. 
Our Commission is made up of members of the 
community and representatives of county agencies, some of 
which are grantee agencies of Area 4 Agency on Aging. 
One 
of our Commission's functions is to make program and fund-
ing recommendations for Area 4. 
Our question is this: 
Should a paid member of a grantee agency 
be allowed to hold an office on the Commission's 
Executive Board? 
We would appreciate any help or information you could 
give us regarding rules or quidelines that we might follow. 
You can reach me at (916) 742-8289 or write to 725 D Street, 
Marysville, CA 
95901. 
Sincerely, 
~ ,:CMut: 
i 
!1 
; 
!" 
Yanet Cornelius 
Commission Secretary

sections.question: Your question is whether the Political Reform Act prevents you from being Secretary of the Commission on Aging.
sections.conclusion: The Political Reform Act does not prevent you from holding the position of Secretary, rather it affects your ability to make and participate in the making of certain decisions. In most instances, decisions regarding grants for the Legal Center require your disqualification under three separate conflict of interest rules: (1) decisions with significant financial effects on non-profit income sources, (2) decisions affecting personal compensation by $100 or more, and (3) decisions with a nexus between the governmental decision and the purposes for which you receive income from the organization.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether an employee of a non-profit grantee organization can serve as Secretary of a Commission on Aging that makes decisions on grants to that organization.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The Political Reform Act does not prohibit the applicant from holding the Secretary position, but requires disqualification from decisions regarding grants to her employer under multiple conflict of interest provisions based on financial interest and nexus tests.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document contains both incoming request (dated May 24, 1982) and FPPC response letter (dated June 23, 1982). Extraction focuses on FPPC response. Minor OCR errors present ('ypu' for 'you', 'aie' for 'are'). References to enclosed Government Code sections and administrative code provisions. Clear question and conclusion sections identifiable.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Your question is whether the Political Reform Act prevents you from being Secretary of the Commission on Aging.

CONCLUSION: The Political Reform Act does not prevent you from holding the position of Secretary, rather it affects your ability to make and participate in the making of certain decisions. In most instances, decisions regarding grants for the Legal Center require your disqualification under three separate conflict of interest rules: (1) decisions with significant financial effects on non-profit income sources, (2) decisions affecting personal compensation by $100 or more, and (3) decisions with a nexus between the governmental decision and the purposes for which you receive income from the organization.

embedding.summary: FPPC advice letter responding to inquiry about whether a paid employee of a non-profit grantee agency can serve as Secretary of a Commission on Aging. FPPC concludes the position is permissible but requires disqualification from grant decisions affecting the employee's employer.
